text: Hope and Resurrection Hope and dread/dispair: Is hope an antidote to all forms of human dread and despair, or is it a form of escapism? How do we relate to “hope” in Christian spirituality, and what is the relationship between “hope” and a resurrection theology? Is resurrection hope cheap triumphalism and a theologia gloriae variant? Our presupposition here is that the resurrection can be part of “thickening alternative faith stories”. A theologia resurrectionis is about reframing life through a radical paradox that Paul recalls from a hymn “Where O death is your victory? Where O death is your sting?” We propose that if pastoral caregiving in the context of the world of today is about change and hope; that the resurrection of Jesus Christ gives an ontology of hope that is capable of transforming us into a new human being. Beyond all the labelling and stigmatising discourses that describe our humanity today; resurrection theology affirms hope in the sense of a new way of being. It means that humanity’s identity is not in our DNA; nor in our gender; nor in our race; nor in our social status. We are identified as human beings by our eschatology – the promise of new creation. Hope care has to do with a new kind of courage to be. A new way of orienting ourselves in a world filled with suffering and different forms of alienation. It is about trying to find alternative frameworks and world views in which to live our human lives in the midst of suffering and all forms of alienation.
explanation: Shows coherent, domain-specific theological argumentation and nuanced phrases (e.g., theologia resurrectionis and thickening alternative faith stories) that are more typical of a human theologian. While advanced AI can imitate this style, the concrete pastoral context and original conceptual framing make human authorship more likely.
